title: Seizures in Neonates
authors: Kayyal S, Inder TE / Fanaroff & Martin 2025

blocks.text:
ทำไม neonatologist ต้องเข้าใจ Seizures in Neonates?
Neonatal seizures เป็น emergency ที่พบบ่อยที่สุดของ neurologic dysfunction ใน NICU — แต่ซับซ้อนเพราะ: (1) 80–90% ของ electrographic seizures ไม่มี clinical signs ที่มองเห็นได้ (Murray Arch Dis Child 2008) → ทำให้ underdiagnose อย่างมากถ้าไม่มี EEG; (2) การรักษาด้วย phenobarbital ปิด motor signs แต่ไม่ปิด EEG seizure (electromechanical uncoupling) → เกิดจาก GABA ที่ยัง excitatory ใน immature cortex (NKCC1 > KCC2); (3) Seizures เองอาจ exacerbate brain injury → PCr/Pi ลดลง 50% ระหว่าง seizure (Younkin Ann Neurol 1986), และ seizure burden correlate กับ MRI injury + worse Bayley scores; (4) 75% ของ neonatal seizures มีสาเหตุจาก 3 entities: HIE (40–50%), stroke (10–15%), ICH (10–20%) — แต่อีก 25% ต้องไม่พลาด metabolic treatable causes (pyridoxine dependency, GLUT1, hypocalcemia); (5) Phenobarbital อาจเป็น neurotoxic ต่อ developing brain → ควร wean เร็วเมื่อ acute seizures resolved.
เป้าหมายการเรียนรู้วันนี้
อธิบาย pathophysiology ของ neonatal seizures: NMDA/GABA imbalance, NKCC1 vs KCC2 mismatch, และ GABA เป็น excitatory ใน immature brain
อธิบาย biochemical consequences ของ seizures: energy metabolism failure, PCr/Pi fall, glucose depletion, และ secondary brain injury mechanism
จำแนก seizure types ตาม 2021 ILAE classification: electroclinical vs electrographic-only; motor (automatisms, clonic, tonic, myoclonic, spasms) + nonmotor
แยก seizures จาก nonepileptic movements: jitteriness (5 criteria), benign neonatal sleep myoclonus, hyperekplexia, pathologic myoclonus
ระบุ seizure etiologies พร้อม relative frequency: HIE, stroke, ICH, infection, metabolic, epilepsy syndromes — และ time of onset ของแต่ละ etiology
อธิบาย metabolic causes ที่รักษาได้: pyridoxine dependency (ALDH7A1/ATQ), PNPO deficiency, GLUT1 defect, hypocalcemia, hypoglycemia — พร้อม specific diagnostic clues
อธิบาย neonatal epilepsy syndromes: BFNE (KCNQ2/3), benign nonfamilial ('fifth-day fits'), EME vs Ohtahara syndrome (burst suppression, etiology, evolution)
ระบุ EEG criteria สำหรับ neonatal seizure diagnosis; ประเมิน aEEG: sensitivity/specificity, limitations, ตำแหน่งใน monitoring hierarchy
ใช้ treatment algorithm: phenobarbital first-line → dosing → second-line options (levetiracetam, phenytoin, lidocaine, midazolam) → pyridoxine trial
ตัดสินใจ duration ของ therapy: 3 prognostic factors, WHO recommendation, risk ของ prolonged phenobarbital exposure, early discontinuation evidence
1. Pathophysiology ของ Neonatal Seizures
1.1 Immature Brain: ทำไม Seizure ง่ายกว่า
Seizure = excessive synchronous electrical discharge ของ neurons → excess Na⁺ influx (depolarization), K⁺ efflux (repolarization); ATP-dependent pump ต้องทำงานมากขึ้น
Excitation >> Inhibition ใน neonatal brain: NMDA และ AMPA receptors เป็น predominant receptors; GABA-A receptors มีน้อยกว่าและยัง excitatory
NMDA ใน neonatal brain มีพฤติกรรมพิเศษ: (1) prolonged EPSP duration; (2) ↓ Mg²⁺ blockade; (3) ↓ inhibitory polyamine sites; (4) ↑ sensitivity to glycine — รวมกัน = hyperexcitable
AMPA ใน neonatal brain: deficient in GluR2 subunit → calcium-permeable → excess excitation
Seizures focal/multifocal เกือบทั้งหมด: secondary generalization หายาก เพราะ immature cortical lamination + incomplete myelination ของ commissures → ต่างจากผู้ใหญ่ที่มี tonic-clonic
1.2 NKCC1 vs KCC2: กุญแจสู่ความเข้าใจ Phenobarbital

GABA เป็น Excitatory ใน Neonatal Brain — NKCC1 > KCC2 Mismatch
Mature brain: GABA-A activation → Cl⁻ influx (KCC2 cotransporter dominant = Cl⁻ efflux maintained; intraneuronal [Cl⁻] low) → hyperpolarization → INHIBITORY. Neonatal brain: NKCC1 (Na⁺-K⁺-Cl⁻ cotransporter, responsible for Cl⁻ INFLUX) is DOMINANT; KCC2 (K⁺-Cl⁻ cotransporter, responsible for Cl⁻ EFFLUX) is low → intraneuronal [Cl⁻] HIGH → GABA-A activation causes Cl⁻ EFFLUX → DEPOLARIZATION → EXCITATION. Maturation: caudal-to-rostral direction → brainstem/spinal cord gain GABA inhibition BEFORE cerebral cortex. Clinical consequence: phenobarbital + benzodiazepines (GABA-A agonists) → suppress motor manifestations (brainstem/spinal cord inhibited) but NOT cortical EEG seizures → electromechanical uncoupling/dissociation. NKCC1 inhibitor bumetanide theoretically corrects this — but phase 1/2 trial (NEMO, Pressler Lancet Neurol 2015) stopped early: hearing impairment in 11/11 survivors, limited efficacy.
1.3 Energy Consequences of Seizures
Seizures → ↑ energy-dependent ion pumping → ↓ ATP + phosphocreatine; ADP ↑ → stimulates glycolysis → NADH ↑ (cytoplasm reduced)
MRS study (Younkin Ann Neurol 1986): 4 neonates seized during ³¹P-MRS imaging → PCr/Pi ↓ 50% ipsilateral to ictal focus; treated with phenobarbital → PCr/Pi recovered immediately

Brain glucose depletes despite normal blood glucose: neonatal rat/monkey models — brain glucose falls to near-undetectable after 30 min of seizure; ↑ lactate (used as fuel but yields only 2 ATP/glucose vs 38 from oxidative phosphorylation)

Secondary brain injury mechanisms: (1) prolonged seizures: hypoventilation/apnea → hypoxemia; cardiac dysfunction → ↓ CBF; (2) excitatory amino acid accumulation (NMDA/AMPA glutamate → reuptake failure) → dendritic/cell body injury especially hippocampus; (3) recurrent seizures: mossy fiber sprouting + ↑ NMDA/AMPA receptors + ↓ GABA receptors → epileptogenesis (Fig 57.3)
CBF paradox: ictal SPECT → 50–150% ↑ regional CBF during seizures (adaptive initially → maladaptive if germinal matrix capillary beds fragile); also ↑ BP from central autonomic component
2. Classification ของ Neonatal Seizures
2.1 ILAE 2021 Framework
2 main types: (1) Electroclinical seizures — EEG seizure + clinical sign correlate; (2) Electrographic-only seizures (subclinical/nonconvulsive/occult) — EEG seizure WITHOUT clinical signs
EEG criteria (ACNS 2013): sudden change in EEG; repetitive waveforms evolving in morphology/frequency/location; amplitude ≥2 µV; duration ≥10 seconds; seizures separated ≥10 sec to count as separate

Clinical recognition is unreliable: Malone et al (Epilepsia 2009): 137 observers → only 50% correctly classified events vs EEG gold standard; Murray et al 2008: only 9% of 526 electrographic seizures identified by bedside staff
2.2 Motor Seizure Types

rows: Coordinated movements: oral-buccal-lingual (sucking, chewing, drooling), oculomotor, pedaling, swimming, rowing
EEG monitoring required — mimic normal behavior; seen in HIE + preterm
Rhythmic jerking, 1–3 Hz (fast phase + slow return), one body region; infant often NOT unconscious
Highest EEG correlate of all clinical types; focal CBF ↑ 50–150%
Migrating jerking (non-Jacksonian pattern: left arm → right leg); several body parts
Consistent EEG correlate; may occur in metabolic encephalopathies
Sustained flexion/extension of one limb or asymmetric trunk; horizontal eye deviation
Decerebrate (ext/ext) or decorticate (flex/ext) posturing; often in severe IVH
85% NO EEG correlate; often brainstem release; poor response to ASM
Rapid isolated flexor jerk, one limb (usually upper)
Usually NO EEG correlate (3/41 had EEG seizure in Mizrahi 1987)
Bilateral flexion jerks; resembles infantile spasms
More likely EEG correlate; seen in severe epileptic syndromes
Proximal + truncal sudden flex/ext; longer than myoclonus, shorter than tonic; grimacing, head nodding variants

blocks.text: 2.3 Nonmotor Seizures + EEG-Only Seizures

Autonomic seizures: alterations of cardiovascular, GI, sudomotor, vasomotor, pupillary, thermoregulatory functions; apnea — but: most apnea in preterm is NON-epileptic; convulsive apnea = less likely to have bradycardia vs nonconvulsive apnea
Behavioral arrest: freezing of activity; rare as isolated type; more commonly part of sequential seizure; requires EEG confirmation
Sequential seizure: variety of signs in sequence, changing lateralization; common in genetic epilepsies (KCNQ2 encephalopathy, self-limited neonatal epilepsies)

EEG-only seizures: 80–90% of all electrographic seizures (Clancy Epilepsia 1988: 79% of 393 seizures were EEG-only); persist after ASM → electromechanical uncoupling; REASON we need continuous EEG monitoring
2.4 Non-Epileptic Movements — Differential Diagnosis

Jitteriness vs Seizure — 5 Distinguishing Features (Table 57.1)
1. Eye findings: Jitteriness = NO ocular phenomena; Seizure = eye fixation/deviation PRESENT. 2. Stimulus sensitivity: Jitteriness = EXQUISITELY stimulus sensitive; Seizure = generally NOT stimulus sensitive. 3. Dominant movement: Jitteriness = TREMOR (equal amplitude, high-frequency, low-amplitude, rhythmic); Seizure = CLONIC JERKING (fast + slow component, 1–3 Hz, decreasing frequency + increasing amplitude over time). 4. Response to passive flexion: Jitteriness STOPS with gentle passive flexion; Seizure does NOT stop. 5. Autonomic changes: Jitteriness = NO autonomic changes; Seizure = POSSIBLE tachycardia, ↑ BP, apnea, salivation, vasomotor changes. Common causes of jitteriness: HIE, hypocalcemia, hypoglycemia, drug withdrawal.

Benign neonatal sleep myoclonus: occurs ONLY during sleep; stops with arousal; can be provoked by gentle rocking; lasts minutes; may be exacerbated by benzodiazepines; EEG normal; resolves ~2 months; neurologic outcome normal
Pathologic myoclonus: brainstem release from loss of cortical inhibition; severe global HI, IVH, drug withdrawal, glycine encephalopathy; abnormal neuro exam + abnormal EEG background

Hyperekplexia (startle disease): exaggerated startle to auditory/visual/somesthetic stimuli + sustained tonic spasms + generalized hypertonia; GLRA1 gene (glycine receptor alpha-1 subunit, chr 5); clonazepam effective; episodes resolve ~2 yrs; AD inheritance
3. Seizure Etiology
3.1 Overall Frequency Distribution

Neonatal Seizure Registry (Glass JPEDS 2016) — 426 neonates with continuous EEG: HIE 38%, ischemic stroke 18%, neonatal onset epilepsy 13%, ICH 11%, genetic epilepsy syndrome 6%, cerebral malformation 4%, BFNE 3%
Summary rule: 3 entities = ~75%: HIE (40–50%), arterial stroke (10–15%), ICH (10–20%); infection 5%, cerebral dysgenesis 5%; metabolic + epilepsy syndromes 5–10%
Seizure burden: 59% had >7 electrographic seizures; 16% had status epilepticus; no significant difference in seizure burden between preterm vs term, or across top 3 etiologies
3.2 Major Structural/Vascular Etiologies

HIE — most common (40–50%): seizures in 48–65% of HIE infants undergoing TH + EEG monitoring; 10–15% → electrographic status epilepticus; usually onset in first 24h; peak 12–24h; may continue during rewarming; EEG background abnormality (burst suppression, attenuation) predicts seizures but no perinatal variable reliably predicts

Ischemic stroke (10–20%): 1/1600–5000 live births; up to 97% of neonatal stroke presents with seizures; 50% seizures = only sign; onset typically AFTER first 12h (later than HIE); infant appears alert between seizures; risk of remote epilepsy 10–54% depending on cohort

Cerebral sinus venous thrombosis (CSVT): 1/8000–38,000/year; 42–78% have venous infarct; seizures in 55–80%; management: correct dehydration/anemia, treat infection; anticoagulation variable (generally tolerated even with ICH)

Intracranial hemorrhage (10–15%): SAH seizures: often well infants with onset day 2 (subarachnoid); GMH-IVH: preterm, first 72h, associated with grade III–IV IVH; subdural: focal seizures, onset first 48h, mostly asymptomatic in modern mild hemorrhages

Intracranial infection (4–5%): equal incidence preterm + term; bacterial: GBS + E. coli meningitis, onset latter part first week; HSV encephalitis: focal, after day 7 (CNS type); TORCH (CMV, toxo): onset days 1–3 if severe; rubella, coxsackievirus B
3.3 Metabolic Disturbances — รักษาได้

rows: Usually day 2; SGA, IDM; ↑ duration → ↑ injury
Dextrose 10% 2 mL/kg IV → maintain GIR up to 8 mg/kg/min
LBW, preterm, IDM; Ca ↓; Mg ↓ often
Calcium gluconate IV; correct Mg if needed
Term, large infant, high-phosphate formula or cow's milk; hypo-Mg; focal clonic on EEG
Calcium + dietary change; check PTH, renal function
Onset hours–days; multifocal clonic; intractable to ASM; EEG: 1–4 Hz bursts + spikes; CSF glycine ↑; PIPECOLIC acid ↑
Pyridoxine 100 mg IV (monitor EEG, prepare for apnea); then 15–30 mg/kg/d oral
Often premature; fetal seizures; does NOT respond to pyridoxine-HCl
Pyridoxal-5-phosphate (PLP) 30 mg/kg/d ÷3 doses (not licensed as drug)
Seizures + low CSF glucose + normal blood glucose; CSF/blood glucose ratio <37%; LOW CSF lactate
Ketogenic diet (provides ketone bodies as alternative fuel)
Burst suppression on EEG; CSF glycine ↑ despite normal plasma; myoclonic; hiccups
Sulfonylurea (NOT insulin alone) → corrects pancreatic + neurologic deficits

blocks.text: Warning signs for inborn errors of metabolism: (1) seizures beginning antepartum; (2) refractory to ASM; (3) progressive clinical/EEG worsening; (4) burst suppression EEG; (5) MRI with prominent brain atrophy; (6) HIE picture without identifiable HI event
3.4 Neonatal Epilepsy Syndromes

5 Neonatal Epilepsy Syndromes — Key Features
1. Benign Familial Neonatal Epilepsy (BFNE): AD; onset day 2–3; focal clonic/tonic/apneic; 10–20/day; self-limited 1–12 months; KCNQ2 (chr 20q13.3) + KCNQ3 (chr 8q24) — voltage-gated K⁺ channels; also PRRT2, SCN2A; 10–15% develop epilepsy later. 2. Benign Nonfamilial Neonatal Convulsions ('Fifth-day fits'): onset day 4–6 (80–90%); multifocal clonic + apnea; status epilepticus in 80%; ALL cease within 15 days; normal diagnostic workup; EEG: 'theta pointu alternant'; de novo KCNQ2 mutations possible. 3. EME (Early Myoclonic Encephalopathy): myoclonic seizures (focal/generalized/erratic) + focal motor + tonic spasms; burst suppression ENHANCED BY SLEEP; persists; etiology = METABOLIC (NKH, amino/organic acids); unfavorable outcome. 4. EIEE/Ohtahara Syndrome: tonic spasms + focal clonic; burst suppression SAME ASLEEP AND AWAKE; evolves to hypsarrhythmia (West syndrome); etiology = STRUCTURAL (dysgenesis); STXBP1 30%, KCNQ2 20%, SCN2A 10%; unfavorable. 5. Malignant Migrating Partial Seizures of Infancy: KCNT1 mutations; migrating foci on EEG; intractable.
4. Diagnosis
4.1 Initial Workup Algorithm
First priority: correctable life-threatening causes: blood glucose (stat) + lumbar puncture for bacterial meningitis; administer dextrose 10% 2 mL/kg if hypoglycemic
Electrolytes: Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, phosphorus, Mg²⁺

Neuroimaging: MRI preferred over cranial US — MRI contributed additional diagnostic info in ~40% of cases beyond US (Weeke Dev Med Child Neurol 2015); many focal lesions not detected by US; cranial US + CT both inferior to MRI for deep nuclear gray matter injury + focal cortical/WM injury (Vermont Oxford Network data)

Metabolic screen if warning signs: plasma amino acids + urine organic acids; CSF glycine (not plasma alone for NKH); CSF lactate (mitochondrial); CSF glucose + blood glucose ratio (<37% → GLUT1); plasma VLFCA (Zellweger)

Genetic testing (rapid WGS): Farnaes NPJ Genom Med 2018: rapid WGS improved health outcomes + net savings in acutely ill infants; useful when genetic condition suspected (family history, refractory seizures, no structural etiology)
4.2 EEG Monitoring — Standards

ACNS 2011 guideline (Shellhaas J Clin Neurophysiol 2011) recommendations: (1) Electrodes: 10-20 system + ECG + respiratory + eye + EMG leads; (2) ≥1 hour recording (at minimum); (3) high-risk neonates: monitor ≥24 hours to screen; (4) if seizures found: continue during ASM management + 24h after last electrographic seizure
Requires trained EEG technologists for electrode application + experienced electroencephalographers for interpretation
Indications for continuous EEG: (1) identify EEG-only seizures; (2) detect electromechanical uncoupling after ASM; (3) distinguish epileptic vs nonepileptic events → avoid unnecessary ASM exposure
4.3 Amplitude-Integrated EEG (aEEG)
Reduced electrode array (2- or 4-channel); compressed display; shows hours of data; upward arches = seizure morphology

Sensitivity/specificity (systematic review, 10 studies; Rakshasbhuvankar Seizure 2015): WITH raw EEG: sensitivity 76% (71–85%), specificity 85% (39–96%); WITHOUT raw EEG: sensitivity 39% (25–80%), specificity 95% (50–100%) → always use with raw EEG tracing
Limitations: misses: low-amplitude seizures, brief seizures, seizures distant from electrodes; not equivalent to full conventional EEG; ACNS: aEEG = 'useful complementary tool' NOT gold standard

Clinical benefit: Shellhaas Pediatr Neurol 2012: aEEG monitoring → greater precision in seizure diagnosis vs clinical signs alone; van Rooij Pediatrics 2010 (RCT n=33): aEEG-guided management → trend toward ↓ seizure exposure + lower seizure burden associated with less MRI injury
5. Prognosis
5.1 Overall Outcomes
Major determinant = underlying neuropathology, ไม่ใช่ seizures เอง (แต่ seizure burden ก็ contributes)

Long-term epilepsy rate: 18% develop epilepsy (Pisani Eur J Paediatr Neurol 2015; 4538 children); 70% epilepsy onset in first year; associated neurologic impairments in 81% (18% intellectual only, 6% CP only, 45% both CP + intellectual)
Preterm: clinical seizures in preterm almost always associated with severe brain injury → poor prognosis
5.2 Prognosis by Etiology (Table 57.5)
5.3 EEG Background as Prognostic Marker
Normal EEG background → normal outcome in 80–100% of neonates with seizures (especially first day of life)
Burst suppression → unfavorable outcome in 80–100%; attenuation → unfavorable 90–100%
Moderate background abnormalities (~15–30% of tracings) → intermediate likelihood of sequelae

Seizure burden and outcome: van Rooij Pediatrics 2010 (RCT, 63 term HIE): positive correlation seizure duration + MRI injury severity (P<0.001); Srinivasakumar Pediatrics 2015 (n=69 HIE): seizure burden → worse MRI scores AND worse Bayley composites (cognitive r=0.502, motor r=0.497, language r=0.444, all P<0.05)
3 clinical determinants for individual prognosis: (1) neonatal neuro exam (abnormal → recurrence risk ~50%); (2) seizure etiology; (3) EEG background pattern
6. Management
6.1 General Principles
WHO guideline 2011: treat ALL clinical and electrographic seizures
Before any ASM: assess and manage ventilation + perfusion; equipment for intubation IMMEDIATELY available; correct hypoglycemia: D10W 2 mL/kg IV (0.2 g/kg) → maintain GIR up to 8 mg/kg/min if needed
Goal of therapy: elimination of ELECTRICAL seizure activity (not just clinical seizures), because most seizures are subclinical

Value of standardized protocol (Harris J Child Neurol 2016): implementation → ↓ max PHB levels (57→41 µg/mL), ↓ progression to SE (46%→36%), ↓ LOS by 10 days; also ↓ unnecessary phenobarbital via improved diagnosis (Wietstock J Child Neurol 2015)
6.2 Treatment Algorithm (Fig 57.6–57.7)

Neonatal Seizure Treatment Algorithm — Step by Step
Step 0 — Confirm seizure with EEG (where available). Check glucose + electrolytes. Start antibiotics if febrile/high-risk CNS infection (LP when stable). Step 1 — PHENOBARBITAL 20 mg/kg IV (over 10–15 min). Start maintenance 5 mg/kg/day divided BID. Check post-load level in 1–2h. Start continuous EEG. Response assessed at 15–20 min. Controls clinical seizures ~40%; electrographic 43–72% (vs 63–80% for clinical endpoint). Step 1b — If seizures continue: additional PHB 20 mg/kg IV. Total ≤40 mg/kg (>40–50 mg/kg: no added benefit, prolonged sedation, cardiovascular toxicity). Step 2 — THREE OPTIONS (choose based on risk/benefit, cardiac status, drug availability): (A) Levetiracetam 50 mg/kg IV → maintenance 40 mg/kg/day ÷2 (preferred at many centers: minimal sedation, no levels needed, SV2a mechanism — different from PHB); (B) Phenytoin/Fosphenytoin 20 mg/kg IV (max 1 mg/kg/min; cardiac monitoring; no dextrose solutions; avoid if channelopathy or cardiac disease); (C) Lidocaine 2 mg/kg IV bolus → 6 mg/kg/h → taper q12h (NOT with phenytoin — arrhythmia risk; NOT cardiac disease; stop within 36h). Special consideration before Step 2: pyridoxine-dependent epilepsy features or intractable → Pyridoxine 100 mg IV (monitor EEG). Step 3 — Midazolam 0.15 mg/kg IV bolus → 1 µg/kg/min → titrate up (max 18 µg/kg/min). Wean after 24h EEG seizure freedom. Step 4 — Pentobarbital drip or Lidocaine drip (if not yet tried).
6.3 Individual Medications
Phenobarbital (First-line)
Loading: 20 mg/kg IV over 10–15 min → target level 20 µg/mL; repeat 20 mg/kg if needed (total ≤40 mg/kg, target 40 µg/mL)
Maintenance: 5 mg/kg/day (accumulates 5–10 days); IV/IM/oral; hepatic + renal excretion affected by HIE
Pharmacokinetics: half-life 43–217h in first week + preterm (→ prolonged NICU stay); rapid CNS entry; CSF/blood ratio 0.58 ± 0.07 at 30 min post-load; accelerated brain entry at local acidosis
Response rates: Gilman Pediatrics 1989: 70% clinical seizure cessation; Painter NEJM 1999: 43% electrographic cessation; NEOLEV2 2020: 90% response if phenobarbital first + HIE + TH

CONCERN: GABA-A agonist → EXCITATORY in immature cortex (NKCC1 > KCC2) → explains only 43% electrographic efficacy; also apoptosis in experimental models (Bittigau Ann NY Acad Sci 2003); Maitre J Perinatol 2013: each 100 mg/kg exposure → 8.1-pt ↓ cognitive, 9-pt ↓ motor Bayley at 24 months; PHB → ↑ CP risk but levetiracetam does not
Levetiracetam
Mechanism: binds SV2a presynaptic vesicle protein → prevents neurotransmitter release; DIFFERENT from PHB/benzos
Dosing: loading 20–60 mg/kg IV; maintenance 40–100 mg/kg/day ÷2–3 doses; no blood levels needed (not correlated with efficacy)
Head-to-head RCT (NEOLEV2, Sharpe Pediatrics 2020): PHB (20–40 mg/kg) 80% seizure control vs levetiracetam (40–60 mg/kg) 28% — PHB significantly better as FIRST-LINE
BUT: reasonable safety profile, ↓ sedation, no drug-drug interactions, IV formulation available; good second-line agent
Higher doses (60 mg/kg) may be more effective; some data suggest neuroprotective properties
Phenytoin/Fosphenytoin
Mechanism: sodium channel blockade (different from PHB/benzos) → USE WHEN channelopathy suspected (KCNQ2/3 BFNE)
Dosing: 20 mg/kg phenytoin equivalents (PE) IV at ≤1 mg/kg/min; cardiac monitoring; NOT in dextrose solutions; fosphenytoin preferred (water soluble, faster rate OK, no tissue injury)
Sequential response (Painter NEJM 1999): PHB alone 43% → adding phenytoin → total ~60% electrographic; ~85% clinical

Complicated pharmacokinetics: highly protein bound (↓ free levels with renal/hepatic disease); nonlinear kinetics; induces hepatic metabolism of PHB; half-life 57h (week 1) → 20h (week 4); must monitor levels
Lidocaine
CNS depressant; mechanism as anticonvulsant uncertain; used more in Europe than North America; 3rd-line at major European centers
Dosing: 2 mg/kg IV bolus → 6 mg/kg/h × 4h → 3.5 mg/kg/h × 12h → 1.75 mg/kg/h × 12h → stop; reduce dose in prematurity + therapeutic hypothermia (↓ clearance)
Efficacy (Weeke Epilepsia 2016, n=413): good/intermediate response 71.4% overall; term > preterm (76% vs 55%); better than midazolam as 2nd-line (21% vs 13%)

CONTRAINDICATION: DO NOT use with phenytoin (↑ arrhythmia risk); avoid if congenital heart disease; stop within 36h (cumulative toxicity); cardiac events 2% (n=521 patients); ↑ risk with unstable K⁺, cardiac dysfunction, concurrent phenytoin
Vitamin B6 — Pyridoxine and PLP
When to trial: (1) clinical/EEG features of B6-dependent epilepsy; (2) seizures intractable to 2nd-line ASM without identified etiology; per 2021 ILAE position paper

Pyridoxine-HCl: 100 mg IV (simultaneous EEG monitoring) or 30 mg/kg/day oral; molecular defect = ALDH7A1 (antiquitin), lysine catabolism pathway; PIPECOLIC acid + α-AASA in urine/plasma/CSF; EEG cessation may take hours; WARN: apnea/hypotonia after infusion (↑ GABA synthesis → brainstem inhibition) → have ventilatory support ready
Pyridoxal-5-phosphate (PLP): 30 mg/kg/day ÷3–6 oral doses; for PNPO deficiency (pyridoxine WILL NOT WORK); also effective for ATQ deficiency; not licensed
Folinic acid (leucovorin): 3–5 mg/kg/day × 3–5 days; for FARS (folinic acid-responsive seizures) = genetically identical to ATQ deficiency; CSF monoamine HPLC peak X
6.4 Duration of Therapy

Risk of subsequent epilepsy: 10–30% overall; refined by 3 factors: (1) abnormal neuro exam at discharge → ~50% recurrence; (2) etiology: cortical dysgenesis ~100%, late hypocalcemia ~0%; (3) EEG background: marked depression → 41% develop epilepsy (Watanabe)
WHO recommendation: consider weaning ASM after 72h if neurologic exam + EEG NORMAL; nature + extent of neuroimaging also guides decision

Evidence for early discontinuation: Guillet J Child Neurol 2007 (n=146): no difference in seizure recurrence or neurologic development at 1–11 years whether PHB continued or stopped at discharge; Glass JAMA Neurol 2021 (multicenter, n=303): no difference in neurodevelopment or epilepsy at 24 months between those who stopped ASM before discharge vs those who continued (for acute symptomatic seizures)

Bottom line: discontinue ASM after resolution of acute symptomatic seizures and prior to hospital discharge — unless high-risk features (malformation, neonatal-onset epileptic encephalopathy, persistently abnormal EEG); wean one drug at a time; PHB = last to discontinue if on multiple ASMs

Neurotoxicity of prolonged PHB: Maitre J Perinatol 2013: each 100 mg/kg PHB → 8.1-pt ↓ cognitive, 9-pt ↓ motor Bayley; PHB associated with CP but levetiracetam not; animal data: apoptotic neurodegeneration within 24h of GABA-A agonists/NMDA blockers in neonatal rat
7. NICU Practical — ทำอะไรเมื่อเห็น Seizure
7.1 Initial Response Checklist
1. Stabilize: check airway/breathing/circulation; prepare for intubation (ASM → respiratory depression)
2. Bedside glucose STAT (heel stick acceptable) → if hypoglycemic: D10W 2 mL/kg IV immediately
3. Call for EEG/aEEG NOW — clinical assessment alone unreliable
4. Draw: electrolytes (Na, Ca, Mg, Phos), blood culture if febrile; LP when stabilized
5. Start antibiotics empirically if febrile or high-risk for CNS infection (do not wait for LP to return)
6. Begin treatment algorithm only after ≥1 EEG-confirmed seizure (where EEG available) and no immediately correctable cause found
7.2 Monitoring Adequacy of Treatment
EEG = only reliable endpoint — clinical cessation after PHB does NOT mean seizure cessation (electromechanical uncoupling in up to 88% of ASM-treated patients)
Check PHB trough level 4–5 days after starting maintenance; repeat post-load level 1–2h post-dose
If clinically apparent seizures cease but EEG continues: do NOT consider treatment successful; escalate ASM
After seizure control: continue EEG monitoring ≥24h of seizure freedom; complete etiologic workup (MRI, metabolic, genetic)
7.3 Electromechanical Uncoupling — ให้ทีม NICU รู้

Electromechanical Uncoupling — สิ่งที่ทุกคนใน NICU ต้องเข้าใจ
Definition: clinical seizures STOP after ASM but electrographic seizures PERSIST on EEG. Mechanism: PHB/benzos (GABA-A agonists) → inhibit brainstem/spinal cord motor neurons FIRST (GABA inhibitory at brainstem level already) → clinical seizure stops → but cortex still EXCITATORY (NKCC1>KCC2) → EEG seizures persist. Frequency: Clancy 1988: 79% of 393 electrical seizures had NO clinical correlate in ASM-treated patients. Implication: Clinical improvement is NOT treatment success. EEG is the only endpoint. Nurse/staff seeing a 'quiet baby' after PHB does NOT mean seizures controlled — they may still be electrographic status epilepticus. Action: request continuous EEG or at minimum aEEG + raw trace for all neonates receiving ASM for seizures.
8. 5 Bedside Pearls

PEARL 1 — 80–90% ของ Electrographic Seizures ไม่มี Clinical Signs: ต้องมี EEG
Murray et al Arch Dis Child 2008 (51 term neonates, continuous video EEG): 9 neonates มี 526 electrographic seizures — only 9% identified by bedside staff as clinical seizures, และอีก 78% ของ nonictal events ถูก misidentify เป็น seizures. นั่นคือ clinical diagnosis ผิดทั้งสองด้าน: under- AND over-diagnosis. Malone Epilepsia 2009 (137 observers): observers correct only 50% of events vs EEG gold standard — ไม่มีใครทำได้ดีกว่า coin flip. ดังนั้น: EEG จำเป็นสำหรับทุก high-risk neonate ที่สงสัย seizures.

PEARL 2 — Phenobarbital: GABA Excitatory ใน Immature Cortex — รู้ Mechanism ไว้
NKCC1 > KCC2 ใน neonatal brain → GABA-A activation → Cl⁻ EFFLUX → DEPOLARIZATION (excitation) ไม่ใช่ inhibition. ดังนั้น PHB ซึ่งเป็น GABA-A agonist จึง: (1) ปิด motor manifestations ได้ (brainstem/spinal cord mature first → GABA inhibitory ที่นั่นแล้ว); (2) ไม่ปิด cortical EEG seizures ได้ดี (immature cortex ยัง excitatory) → electromechanical uncoupling. ผลคือ PHB controls clinical seizures ใน 70–80% แต่ controls electrographic seizures ใน เพียง 43%. ต้อง EEG เพื่อดู true efficacy.

PEARL 3 — Pyridoxine Dependency: ลองก่อนพลาดแชนซ์รักษาหาย
Pyridoxine-dependent epilepsy (ALDH7A1/ATQ deficiency) = seizures intractable to standard ASM + onset in first hours of life + EEG showing 1–4 Hz bursts + spikes. วิธี dx: diagnostic trial of pyridoxine 100 mg IV ขณะ monitoring EEG (expect apnea/hypotonia → have IPPV ready). Confirmatory: PIPECOLIC acid + α-AASA in urine/CSF; ALDH7A1 gene. ถ้าไม่ response: ลอง pyridoxal-5-phosphate 30 mg/kg/day oral (สำหรับ PNPO deficiency). ถ้ายัง: ลอง folinic acid 3–5 mg/kg/day. Rule: ทุก neonate ที่ seizures refractory ไม่มี known etiology = ควร Vitamin B6 trial.

PEARL 4 — Lidocaine + Phenytoin = CONTRAINDICATED ร่วมกัน
ทั้ง lidocaine และ phenytoin มี pro-arrhythmic properties — combination → ↑ arrhythmia + cardiac arrest risk. Weeke Epilepsia 2016 (521 patients): cardiac events in 2%; risk factors = unstable K⁺, cardiac dysfunction, concurrent phenytoin. ดังนั้น algorithm: ถ้าใช้ phenytoin/fosphenytoin แล้ว → ห้ามใช้ lidocaine; ถ้าจะใช้ lidocaine → ห้ามใช้ phenytoin ก่อน. Lidocaine เหมาะสม: epilepsy refractory ที่ไม่มี cardiac disease และไม่ได้รับ phenytoin. หยุดภายใน 36h เสมอ.

PEARL 5 — Wean ASM ก่อน Discharge ถ้า Acute Symptomatic Seizures: เหมาะสมและ Safe
Glass JAMA Neurol 2021 (multicenter, 303 neonates, acute symptomatic seizures): early discontinuation ก่อน discharge ไม่ได้ increase seizure recurrence หรือ worse neurodevelopment ที่ 24 months vs ยังคง ASM ต่อไป. ทำไมสำคัญ: prolonged PHB exposure → Bayley ↓ 8 points/100 mg/kg (cognitive) และ 9 points (motor); associated with CP. WHO guideline: wean after 72h ถ้า neuro exam + EEG normal. Practice: wean ทีละตัว, PHB = ตัวสุดท้าย; ถ้า malformation/genetic epilepsy syndrome → ไม่ wean ก่อน f/u ชัดเจน.
9. Evidence Update — Key Studies

Murray DM Arch Dis Child Fetal Neonatal Ed 2008;93:F187: continuous video EEG 51 term neonates: only 9% of 526 electrographic seizures identified by staff; 78% of nonictal events misidentified — definitive study showing clinical seizure identification is unreliable
Malone A Epilepsia 2009;50:2097: 137 observers, 20 neonates: interobserver agreement poor; only 50% of events correctly classified vs EEG — even expert clinicians at 50% accuracy
Clancy RR Epilepsia 1988;29:256: 79% of 393 EEG seizures were occult; 88% of patients already on ASM — electromechanical uncoupling after ASM treatment
Dzhala VI Nat Med 2005;11:1205: NKCC1 transporter facilitates seizures in developing brain; NKCC1>KCC2 → GABA excitatory in neonatal brain — mechanistic basis for limited PHB efficacy
Younkin DP Ann Neurol 1986;20:513: ³¹P-MRS during neonatal seizures: PCr/Pi ↓ 50% ipsilateral to ictal focus; phenobarbital → immediate PCr/Pi recovery — established energy failure during seizures

Painter MJ NEJM 1999;341:485: RCT phenobarbital vs phenytoin for neonatal seizures: PHB 43% vs PHT 45% complete electrographic control; combined ~80% at 80% reduction — established equivalence but limited efficacy
Sharpe C (NEOLEV2) Pediatrics 2020;145:e20193182: RCT LEV (40–60 mg/kg) vs PHB (20–40 mg/kg): PHB 80% vs LEV 28% seizure control — PHB superior as first-line despite neurotoxicity concern

Weeke LC Epilepsia 2016;57:233: lidocaine retrospective 413 neonates (319 term + 94 preterm, aEEG confirmed): good/intermediate response 71.4%; term > preterm (76% vs 55%); lidocaine better than midazolam as 2nd-line
Pressler RM (NEMO) Lancet Neurol 2015;14:469: bumetanide + PHB for HIE seizures: stopped early — hearing impairment ALL 11 survivors; limited efficacy — bumetanide not currently recommended
van Rooij LG Pediatrics 2010;125:e358: RCT aEEG-guided management (n=33): trend toward ↓ seizure exposure; ↓ seizure burden → less MRI injury — supporting EEG monitoring for seizure management

Srinivasakumar P Pediatrics 2015;136:e1302: RCT 69 HIE: EEG-guided management → ↓ seizure burden + worse MRI injury + worse Bayley (cognitive r=0.502, motor r=0.497, language r=0.444) with ↑ seizure burden — supports treating electrographic seizures
Glass HC JAMA Neurol 2021;78:817: multicenter 303 neonates acute symptomatic seizures: early ASM discontinuation before discharge = safe; no difference in epilepsy or neurodevelopment at 24 months
Maitre NL J Perinatol 2013;33:841: 280 neonates: PHB exposure → 8.1-pt ↓ cognitive + 9-pt ↓ motor Bayley/100 mg/kg (P=0.01); LEV also worsened scores but less; PHB → ↑ CP but LEV did not
Glass HC J Pediatr 2016;174:98: Neonatal Seizure Registry 426 neonates continuous EEG: HIE 38%, stroke 18%, epilepsy 13%, ICH 11%, genetic 6%, malformation 4%, BFNE 3%; 59% had >7 seizures, 16% SE
Shellhaas RA J Clin Neurophysiol 2011;28:611: ACNS guideline continuous EEG monitoring in neonates: standardized criteria, monitoring duration, electrode placement — current clinical standard
Rakshasbhuvankar A Seizure 2015;33:90: systematic review aEEG for neonatal seizures: with raw EEG sensitivity 76%/specificity 85%; without raw EEG sensitivity 39% — raw EEG essential

Bittigau P Ann NY Acad Sci 2003;993:103: GABA-A agonists + NMDA blockers (PHB, PHT, diazepam, clonazepam, valproate, ketamine) → apoptotic neurodegeneration in developing rat brain within 24h; combinations worse

Singh NA Brain 2003;126:2726: KCNQ2 + KCNQ3 mutations in BFNE: expansion of mutation spectrum; both encode voltage-gated K⁺ channels, form heteromeric complex regulating neuronal excitability threshold
Harris ML J Child Neurol 2016: standardized SE treatment protocol: ↓ max PHB levels (57→41 µg/mL), ↓ progression to SE (46%→36%), ↓ LOS by 10 days — value of protocolized management
10. Self-Check Q&A
Q: ทำไม GABA ถึงเป็น excitatory ใน neonatal brain และมีผลต่อ phenobarbital อย่างไร?

ตอบ: NKCC1 (Na⁺-K⁺-Cl⁻ cotransporter) dominant > KCC2 (K⁺-Cl⁻) ใน neonatal brain → intraneuronal [Cl⁻] สูง → GABA-A activation → Cl⁻ EFFLUX (ออกจาก cell) → DEPOLARIZATION → EXCITATION. Maturation caudal-to-rostral: brainstem/spinal cord gain GABA inhibition before cortex → PHB (GABA-A agonist) → inhibits motor manifestations (brainstem inhibited) but NOT cortical EEG seizures (cortex still excitatory) → electromechanical uncoupling. ผล: PHB controls clinical seizures ~70–80% แต่ electrographic seizures เพียง 43%.
Q: แยก jitteriness จาก seizure ด้วย 5 clinical features

ตอบ: 1. Eye: Jitteriness = no ocular phenomena; Seizure = eye deviation/fixation PRESENT. 2. Stimulus: Jitteriness = exquisitely stimulus sensitive; Seizure = not sensitive. 3. Movement: Jitteriness = tremor (equal amplitude, high-frequency, low-amplitude); Seizure = clonic jerking (fast+slow component, 1–3 Hz, ↓ frequency + ↑ amplitude over time). 4. Passive flexion: Jitteriness STOPS; Seizure does NOT stop. 5. Autonomic: Jitteriness = absent; Seizure = may have tachycardia, ↑BP, apnea, salivation. Causes of jitteriness: HIE, hypocalcemia, hypoglycemia, drug withdrawal.
Q: ระบุ top 3 etiologies ของ neonatal seizures พร้อม approximate frequency และ time of onset

ตอบ: 1. HIE (40–50%): onset first 24h (peak 12–24h); may continue during TH rewarming; 48–65% of HIE with EEG have seizures. 2. Ischemic stroke (10–15%): onset typically AFTER first 12h (later than HIE); up to 97% of neonatal stroke presents with seizures; infant appears alert between seizures; 1/1600–5000 live births. 3. ICH (10–20%): GMH-IVH = preterm, first 72h, severe grades; SAH = day 2, usually well between seizures; subdural = focal, first 48h. Together these 3 = ~75% of all neonatal seizures.
Q: Describe the phenobarbital treatment algorithm for neonatal seizures including doses and expected response rates

ตอบ: Step 1: PHB 20 mg/kg IV → response at 15–20 min → controls clinical seizures ~40%, electrographic ~43%. Step 1b: additional PHB 20 mg/kg (total ≤40 mg/kg) → clinical ~70%, electrographic ~60%. Maintenance: 5 mg/kg/day (accumulates 5–10 days). Step 2 (3 options): (A) levetiracetam 50 mg/kg IV (preferred: ↓ sedation, no levels, different mechanism); (B) phenytoin/fosphenytoin 20 mg/kg (sodium channel, use if channelopathy; NOT with lidocaine); (C) lidocaine 2 mg/kg bolus → 6 mg/kg/h taper (NOT with phenytoin, stop ≤36h). Special: pyridoxine 100 mg IV if features of B6-dependent epilepsy or intractable to 2nd-line. Step 3: midazolam drip. Step 4: pentobarbital/lidocaine drip.
Q: BFNE vs Ohtahara syndrome vs EME — compare etiology, EEG, evolution, prognosis

ตอบ: BFNE: KCNQ2/3 (AD); onset day 2–3; focal clonic/tonic/apneic; NORMAL background between seizures; self-limited 1–12 months; normal development (10–15% → epilepsy later). Ohtahara (EIEE): STXBP1/KCNQ2/SCN2A; onset first weeks; TONIC SPASMS + focal clonic; BURST SUPPRESSION same asleep+awake; evolves to HYPSARRHYTHMIA (West); structural/genetic etiology; unfavorable. EME: METABOLIC (NKH mainly); onset first weeks; MYOCLONIC + focal clonic; BURST SUPPRESSION ENHANCED BY SLEEP (not identical to Ohtahara); persists (no hypsarrhythmia); metabolic etiology predominant; unfavorable.
Q: ระบุ clinical clues ของ pyridoxine-dependent epilepsy และ approach การวินิจฉัย + รักษา

ตอบ: Clinical clues: onset hours of life; multifocal clonic; refractory to ALL standard ASM; hyperalertness/tremulousness; EEG: generalized 1–4 Hz bursts + spikes (unusual paroxysmal pattern); maternal awareness of fetal seizures. Diagnostic trial: pyridoxine 100 mg IV + simultaneous EEG monitoring; warn team: apnea/hypotonia may occur (GABA surge → brainstem inhibition) → have IPPV ready. EEG normalization may take hours. Confirm: plasma pipecolic acid + urine/CSF α-AASA; ALDH7A1 gene. Long-term: 15–30 mg/kg/day oral pyridoxine. If no response: try PLP (PNPO deficiency). If still no response: folinic acid.
Q: GLUT1 defect: ทำไม CSF glucose ต่ำ แต่ blood glucose ปกติ และ treatment คืออะไร?

ตอบ: Mechanism: SLC2A1 mutation (AD, heterozygous) → ↓ GLUT1 transporter → impaired facilitative diffusion of glucose across blood-brain barrier + neuronal membrane → ↓ CSF glucose. Lab pattern: low CSF glucose + NORMAL blood glucose; CSF/blood glucose ratio mean 37%; LOW CSF lactate (not elevated — not increased glycolysis, just impaired transport). Blood glucose normal because systemic glucose metabolism unaffected. Treatment: ketogenic diet → supplies ketone bodies (transported independently of GLUT1) as alternative brain fuel → seizure control; blunts impaired neurologic development.
Q: ตัดสินใจ duration ของ ASM therapy อย่างไรหลัง neonatal seizures?

ตอบ: 3 prognostic factors: (1) neonatal neuro exam at discharge (abnormal → recurrence risk ~50%); (2) etiology (dysgenesis ~100% epilepsy, late hypocalcemia ~0%); (3) EEG background (marked depression → 41% develop epilepsy). Evidence for early discontinuation: Glass JAMA Neurol 2021 (303 neonates): no difference in neurodevelopment/epilepsy whether stopped before discharge vs continued — supports early discontinuation for acute symptomatic seizures. WHO: consider weaning at 72h if exam + EEG normal. Wean one drug at a time; PHB last. Prolonged PHB → Maitre 2013: ↓ Bayley 8 pts/100 mg/kg; ↑ CP risk → minimize exposure.
Q: Electromechanical uncoupling: อธิบาย mechanism และ implications

ตอบ: Definition: clinical seizures STOP after ASM, but EEG seizures PERSIST. Mechanism: PHB/benzos (GABA-A agonists) → inhibit brainstem/spinal cord (GABA already inhibitory there — caudal regions mature first) → motor manifestations stop; cortex remains excitatory (NKCC1>KCC2) → EEG seizures persist. Frequency: Clancy 1988: 79% of 393 EEG seizures had no clinical correlate (88% of patients had already received ASM). Clinical implication: 'quiet baby after PHB' ≠ seizure controlled; only EEG confirms adequate treatment. Must have EEG monitoring to assess adequacy.
Q: aEEG: sensitivity/specificity และเมื่อไหร่ที่ inadequate?

ตอบ: Sensitivity + specificity (Rakshasbhuvankar Seizure 2015, 10 studies): WITH raw EEG: sensitivity 76% (71–85%), specificity 85% (39–96%); WITHOUT raw EEG: sensitivity ↓ to 39% (25–80%), specificity 95% (50–100%). Misses: low-amplitude seizures, brief seizures, seizures DISTANT from electrodes. ACNS position: aEEG = useful complementary tool NOT gold standard → always use WITH raw EEG tracing. Full conventional EEG remains gold standard for accurate seizure identification and management decisions.
Q: Walker-Warburg syndrome (WWS) vs BFNE — สรุปความแตกต่างสำคัญ

ตอบ: WWS: cobblestone type II lissencephaly; POMT1/POMT2 mutations; O-glycosylation defect → ↓ alpha-dystroglycan → defective glial-limiting membrane → neurons over-migrate → cobblestone; HARD±E (Hydrocephalus + Agyria + Retinal Dysplasia ± Encephalocele + muscular dystrophy); die within first months; severe seizures. BFNE: AD; KCNQ2/3 (voltage-gated K⁺ channels); onset day 2–3; NORMAL interictal neuro exam; self-limited; normal development (10–15% develop later epilepsy); genetic counseling for family. These represent opposite ends of prognosis spectrum for neonatal epilepsy.

Day 155 Preview — Fanaroff Ch.58
บทต่อไป: Chapter 58 — Hypotonia and Neuromuscular Disease in the Neonate. เตรียมตัว: floppy infant DDx: central vs peripheral hypotonia (acute HIE/metabolic vs SMA/congenital myopathy/NMJ disorders); spinal muscular atrophy (SMA) type 0/1: SMN1 deletion, absent deep tendon reflexes, tongue fasciculations, respiratory failure — newborn screening; congenital myotonic dystrophy: maternal CTG repeat, neonatal hypotonia + respiratory failure, feeding difficulty, congenital clubfoot; Prader-Willi vs neonatal HIE: distinguishing features; botulism, myasthenia gravis: NMJ disorders presenting as floppy newborn.
